steps.title: Find the textual evidence for Tía Ana's motivation
steps.content:
The text establishes that the front parlor is Tía Ana's "special province" for her school. The salon guests use it each evening, leaving it disordered for her class the next morning. She comes into the room several times to check whether the guests have left. This repeated checking points to one motive: she wants the room back for her school.
  • Choice A — She considers the guests to be uninteresting and is trying to convince them to leave.: the text gives no indication she finds them uninteresting; her concern is the room, not the guests themselves ✗
  • Choice B — She is impatient to share her plans to start a new school with the guests and hopes they will support her.: the school already exists and there is no mention of seeking the guests' support ✗
  • Choice C — She is anxious for the gathering to disperse so that she can ready the space for her own needs.: matches her repeated checking and the established context of the disordered classroom ✓
  • Choice D — She is frustrated because she needs assistance elsewhere in the house, but Salomé is unavailable while entertaining the guests.: the text does not mention a need elsewhere or Salomé's unavailability as Tía Ana's concern ✗

steps.title: Confirm the answer
steps.content: Tía Ana's repeated checking is explained entirely by her desire to reclaim the parlor for her school — nothing more. That matches Choice C — She is anxious for the gathering to disperse so that she can ready the space for her own needs..
